name: fp32 / float32
oneliner: The standard 32-bit floating-point format — baseline 'full precision' for neural networks.
explanation: Each parameter stored as 4 bytes. A 7B model = 28 GB. Provides ~7.2 decimal digits of precision and range up to ±3.4×10^38. Now considered overkill for inference; still used for optimizer states and master weight copies in mixed-precision training. When people say a model is 'quantized,' they mean converted from fp32/bf16 to something smaller.
fundamentals: Bit layout: 1 sign | 8 exponent (bias 127) | 23 mantissa. Value = (-1)^sign × 2^(exp-127) × 1.mantissa. The 23-bit mantissa + implicit leading 1 = 24 bits of significand ≈ 7.2 decimal digits. Special values: ±inf (exp all 1s, mantissa 0), NaN (exp all 1s, mantissa ≠0), subnormals (exp all 0s).

name: fp16 / float16
oneliner: A 16-bit float that halves memory vs fp32 but has limited dynamic range — fast for inference, needs loss scaling for training.
explanation: Cuts memory in half (7B model = 14 GB). Enables Tensor Cores on NVIDIA Volta+ GPUs for 2-8× throughput. Critical limitation: max value is only 65,504 — gradients during training can overflow, requiring loss scaling. For inference, overflow is rarely an issue. Largely supplanted by bf16 for training where hardware supports it.
fundamentals: Bit layout: 1 sign | 5 exponent (bias 15) | 10 mantissa. Value = (-1)^sign × 2^(exp-15) × 1.mantissa. Range: ±65,504. Precision: ~3.3 decimal digits. Compared to fp32: range drops from ~10^38 to ~6.5×10^4 (34 orders of magnitude less), precision from ~7.2 to ~3.3 digits.
sources: Micikevicius et al., 'Mixed Precision Training,' ICLR 2018, arXiv:1710.03740

foundational_papers.title: Mixed Precision Training

name: bf16 / bfloat16
oneliner: A 16-bit float with fp32's exponent range but reduced mantissa — designed for deep learning training without loss scaling.
explanation: Developed by Google Brain for TPUs (2018). Keeps fp32's 8-bit exponent so it has the same range (±3.4×10^38) — gradients almost never overflow, no loss scaling needed. Tradeoff: only 7-bit mantissa (vs fp16's 10), so less precise than fp16. In practice, the reduced precision doesn't matter for neural nets. Default training format for LLMs at Google, Meta, and most labs. Supported on TPUs, NVIDIA Ampere+, AMD MI200+.
fundamentals: Bit layout: 1 sign | 8 exponent (bias 127) | 7 mantissa. Same exponent as fp32 — conversion is just truncating the lower 16 bits. Precision: ~2.4 decimal digits (vs fp16's ~3.3). Two values differing by less than 1 part in 128 map to the same bf16.
sources: Kalamkar et al., 'A Study of BFLOAT16 for Deep Learning Training,' arXiv:1905.12322

foundational_papers.title: A Study of BFLOAT16 for Deep Learning Training

name: fp8 (E4M3 / E5M2)
expansion: 8-bit Floating Point — two variants for forward and backward passes
oneliner: Two 8-bit float formats: E4M3 for weights/activations (more precision) and E5M2 for gradients (more range). 2× throughput vs fp16.
explanation: Joint proposal by NVIDIA, ARM, Intel (2022). E4M3 (4 exp, 3 mantissa): max value 448, ~1.2 decimal digits — for forward pass. E5M2 (5 exp, 2 mantissa): max value 57,344, ~0.9 digits — for backward pass gradients. Native on H100/H200 Tensor Cores at 2× fp16 throughput. NVIDIA Transformer Engine auto-manages per-tensor scaling. For inference, E4M3 only (no gradients). Typically <0.1% accuracy loss vs fp16.
fundamentals: E4M3: 1 sign | 4 exp (bias 7) | 3 mantissa. No infinity — all exp=1111 patterns are finite (deliberate departure from IEEE 754). Only 16 values per sign per binade. E5M2: 1 sign | 5 exp (bias 15) | 2 mantissa. Follows IEEE 754 conventions (has inf/NaN). Per-tensor scaling factors are essential — without them, clipping destroys quality.
sources: Micikevicius et al., 'FP8 Formats for Deep Learning,' arXiv:2209.05433

foundational_papers.title: FP8 Formats for Deep Learning

name: int8 / i8
oneliner: Fixed-point 8-bit: maps floats to 256 discrete levels via scale+zero-point. 4× compression from fp32, runs on all modern hardware.
explanation: Most mature quantization format. 256 levels (signed: -128 to 127). Integer MAC units are simpler and more efficient than float, available on CPUs (VNNI), GPUs (INT8 Tensor Cores since Turing), mobile NPUs. Challenge: LLM activations can have extreme outliers in certain channels (documented in LLM.int8() paper). Solutions: per-channel quantization, mixed-precision decomposition, SmoothQuant. Standard for production W8A8 serving via TensorRT-LLM.
fundamentals: Quantize: x_q = round(x / scale) + zero_point, clamped to [-128, 127]. Dequantize: x ≈ (x_q - zero_point) × scale. Symmetric (common for weights): zero_point=0, scale = max(|x|)/127. Step size = scale. For weights in [-1, 1]: step ≈ 0.0078 — values closer than ~0.008 become identical.

foundational_papers.title: LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale
SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for LLMs

name: int4 / i4
oneliner: Just 16 discrete levels per value — 8× compression from fp32. Makes 30B+ models runnable on consumer GPUs.
explanation: A 70B model goes from 140 GB (fp16) to ~35 GB (int4). Naive int4 destroys quality — per-group quantization is essential: weights divided into groups of 32-128, each with its own scale factor. With group size 128, overhead is only 0.125 bits/weight (effective 4.125 bpw). Standard format for local/consumer inference via llama.cpp, GPTQ, AWQ. Activations kept in fp16 (W4A16). Quality: +0.1-1.0 perplexity points; works better on larger models (more redundancy).
fundamentals: Signed: -8 to 7 (two's complement in 4 bits). Two values packed per byte. Per-group storage (g=128): 128×4 bits + 16 bits (fp16 scale) + optional 4 bits (zero-point) = 4.125 effective bpw. Step size for range [-1,1]: 2/15 ≈ 0.133 — 17× coarser than int8.

resources.label: GPTQ paper (foundational for int4 LLM use)

name: nf4 / NormalFloat4
oneliner: Non-uniform 4-bit format with levels spaced at quantiles of a Gaussian — optimal for neural network weights, used by QLoRA/bitsandbytes.
explanation: Introduced in the QLoRA paper (Dettmers 2023). Neural network weights ≈ Gaussian when normalized. Uniform int4 wastes levels in sparse tails and under-resolves the dense center. nf4 places 16 levels at N(0,1) quantiles so each level covers equal probability mass (1/16). Reduces quantization error vs uniform int4 for the same 4-bit budget. With 'double quantization' (quantizing scale factors to fp8), effective ~4.127 bpw. Default in bitsandbytes load_in_4bit.
fundamentals: Lookup table: {-1.0, -0.6962, -0.5251, -0.3949, -0.2844, -0.1848, -0.0911, 0.0, 0.0796, 0.1609, 0.2461, 0.3379, 0.4407, 0.5626, 0.7230, 1.0}. Levels are denser near zero (spacing 0.08) and sparser in tails (spacing 0.28). Quantize: normalize per group w_norm = w/absmax, map to nearest level. Dequantize: nf4_lookup[index] × absmax.

foundational_papers.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models

name: E2M1 (MXFP4)
oneliner: 4-bit micro-float: only 15 distinct values, made viable by sharing an 8-bit block scale across 32 elements. Next-gen format for Blackwell GPUs.
explanation: Part of the OCP Microscaling (MX) spec (AMD, ARM, Intel, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, Qualcomm, 2023). Standalone E2M1 is nearly useless — 15 values. The MX trick: 32 E2M1 elements share one E8M0 scale (8-bit exponent, no mantissa). Scale handles magnitude, E2M1 handles relative differences within the block. 2× compute density vs fp8. NVIDIA Blackwell has native MXFP4 Tensor Cores. Also defines MXFP6 (E3M2/E2M3) and MXFP8.
fundamentals: E2M1: 1 sign | 2 exp (bias 1) | 1 mantissa. No inf/NaN. Positive values: 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 6.0. With sign: 15 distinct values. E8M0 block scale: pure power of 2 (2^(exp-127)), range 2^-127 to 2^127. Effective value = E2M1_value × 2^(shared_exp - 127). Effective bpw: 4 + 8/32 = 4.25 bits.

foundational_papers.title: OCP Microscaling Formats (MX) Specification v1.0
foundational_papers.authors: OCP (AMD, ARM, Intel, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, Qualcomm)

foundational_papers.title: Microscaling Data Formats for Deep Learning

name: Quantization
oneliner: Converting a model's high-precision floats to lower-precision representations — the single most impactful optimization for LLM serving.
explanation: Maps weights (and optionally activations/KV-cache) from fp32/bf16/fp16 to int8/int4/fp8/etc. Core operation: find the nearest representable value in the target format. Why it matters: LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound. Generating one token reads ALL weights from memory. A 70B fp16 model (140 GB) on an H100 (3.35 TB/s) = ~42ms/token minimum. At int4 (35 GB) = ~10ms/token. Quality impact: fp8 ≈ lossless; int8 ≈ lossless; int4 = +0.1-1.0 perplexity; below 4-bit = requires specialized methods.
fundamentals: Uniform affine quantization: x_q = clamp(round(x/s + z), q_min, q_max). Dequantize: x̂ = s×(x_q - z). Scale s = (x_max - x_min)/(q_max - q_min). Symmetric: z=0, s = max(|x|)/q_max. Error per value bounded by s/2. Not all weights contribute equally — attention layers and first/last layers are typically more sensitive.
sources: Gholami et al., 'A Survey of Quantization Methods,' arXiv:2103.13630

foundational_papers.title: A Survey of Quantization Methods for Efficient Neural Network Inference

name: W4A16
oneliner: Weight-only quantization: weights stored in 4-bit, dequantized to fp16 on-the-fly for computation. Saves memory, compute stays fp16.
explanation: The standard for memory-constrained inference (consumer GPUs, llama.cpp). Weights read from memory in 4-bit (small, fast), dequantized in registers to fp16, then standard fp16 GEMM. Saves memory bandwidth but compute is still fp16 — no compute speedup for large batches. Contrast with W8A8 where both operands are quantized and integer math provides both memory and compute savings.
fundamentals: Inference per layer: 1) Read int4 weights from memory. 2) Dequantize: W_fp16 = scale × (W_int4 - zero_point). 3) GEMM: Y = X_fp16 @ W_fp16. Memory bandwidth savings: 4× vs fp16. Compute savings: none (still fp16 GEMM). For batch=1 (memory-bound): ~4× throughput. For large batches (compute-bound): no speedup.

name: W8A8
oneliner: Both weights and activations quantized to 8-bit — saves memory AND compute via integer arithmetic. Standard for data-center serving.
explanation: Both operands of the matrix multiply are int8, enabling INT8 GEMM with INT32 accumulation. Provides both memory bandwidth savings (2× vs fp16) and compute savings (INT8 Tensor Cores are faster). Requires quantizing activations at runtime, which is harder due to input-dependent distributions and outliers. Standard for production serving via TensorRT-LLM, vLLM. SmoothQuant makes this viable by mathematically migrating quantization difficulty from activations to weights.
fundamentals: Inference: 1) Quantize activation: X_int8 = round(X_fp16/s_x). 2) Read int8 weights. 3) INT8 GEMM: Y_int32 = X_int8 @ W_int8. 4) Dequantize: Y_fp16 = Y_int32 × s_x × s_w. Key difference from W4A16: saves both bandwidth AND compute. For large batch sizes (compute-bound workloads), W8A8 provides real speedup while W4A16 does not.

name: Calibration Data
oneliner: A small dataset (~128 samples) run through the model to collect activation statistics needed by quantization methods to set scale factors.
explanation: Some methods need to observe actual activation ranges (which depend on input data) to set quantization parameters. Standard: 128 samples × 2048 tokens from C4 or WikiText-2 (established by GPTQ paper). Required by: GPTQ (Hessian computation), AWQ (activation importance), SmoothQuant (smoothing factors), static quantization. NOT required by: simple RTN, dynamic quantization, HQQ. Domain-matched calibration (e.g., code for code models) yields slightly better results.
fundamentals: For layer Y=XW+b: calibration records per-layer activation stats. Approaches: min/max (simple but outlier-sensitive), percentile (clips outliers), MSE-optimal (minimizes reconstruction error), entropy/KL-based (TensorRT). For GPTQ: calibration computes Hessian H = 2XX^T, used to solve layer-wise optimization min_Q ||WX - QX||^2.

resources.label: White Paper on Neural Network Quantization

name: Per-Group Quantization
oneliner: Divides weights into small groups (32-128 values) with separate scale factors per group — essential for making int4 viable.
explanation: Granularity spectrum: per-tensor (1 scale for whole matrix, coarse) → per-channel (1 per output row) → per-group (1 per g values, fine). Per-group is essential for int4 where 16 levels need to fit local distributions. For a [4096, 4096] weight matrix with g=128: 131K scale factors, 256 KB overhead, +0.125 effective bpw. Tradeoff: finer groups = better accuracy but more overhead. Int8: per-channel usually sufficient. Int4: g=128 minimum, g=32 noticeably better.
fundamentals: Error depends on range: scale = range/(2^b - 1), max error = scale/2. Smaller groups have smaller local ranges → smaller scales → smaller errors. Overhead for g=128 with fp16 scales: 16/128 = 0.125 bits/weight. For g=32: 16/32 = 0.5 bits/weight.

resources.label: White Paper on Neural Network Quantization

name: PTQ
oneliner: Quantize an already-trained model without further training — fast (minutes to hours), practical, dominates LLM quantization.
explanation: Takes a fully trained model and converts to lower precision in one pass. No training infrastructure needed. Methods: GPTQ, AWQ, RTN, EXL2. Fast even for 70B models. Downside: model never adapted to quantization noise, so quality degrades more than QAT, especially below 4-bit. At int8: essentially lossless. At int4: measurable but acceptable. At int2: usually unacceptable without QAT.
fundamentals: Workflow: 1) Load fp16/bf16 model. 2) Optional calibration pass. 3) Compute scale/zero-point per layer/channel/group. 4) Quantize: W_q = round(W/scale) + zp. 5) Save. At inference: dequantize and compute.

resources.label: White Paper on Neural Network Quantization

name: QAT
oneliner: Simulate quantization during training so the model learns to be robust to quantization noise — better quality than PTQ but requires full training infrastructure.
explanation: Forward pass uses 'fake quantization' (quantize then immediately dequantize, introducing realistic noise). Gradients flow through rounding via Straight-Through Estimator (STE). Over many steps, weights adapt to quantize well. Quality gap vs PTQ widens at lower bits: at int8 no benefit, at int4 meaningful, at int2 essential. Cost: requires training/fine-tuning infrastructure (hundreds of GPUs for 70B). Meta and Google have published LLM QAT results. Google's Gemma uses QAT for official quantized variants.
fundamentals: Forward: W_fake_q = dequantize(quantize(W)), Y = X @ W_fake_q. Backward: STE approximates d(round(x))/dx ≈ 1 — gradients pass through as if rounding wasn't there. Optimizer updates full-precision master weights. The model learns weights that happen to quantize well.

foundational_papers.title: Quantization and Training of Neural Networks for Efficient Integer-Arithmetic-Only Inference

name: Dequantization
oneliner: Reverse mapping from low-precision quantized values back to approximate floats — happens on-the-fly during inference.
explanation: In W4A16: int4 weights read from memory, multiplied by group scale in registers/shared memory, fed directly into fp16 GEMM. Fused with computation to avoid materializing full fp16 weight matrix — only 4 bits traverse the memory bus. In W8A8: dequantization happens AFTER the integer GEMM (output × scale product), which is cheaper since it operates on the smaller output tensor. Dequantization is cheap: O(KN) vs O(MKN) for the GEMM itself.
fundamentals: Integer: W_approx = scale × (W_q - zero_point). Error: |W_original - W_approx| ≤ scale/2. nf4: W_approx = nf4_codebook[index] × absmax. W8A8 output: Y_fp16 = Y_int32 × (s_x × s_w).

name: Model Size ↔ Memory
expansion: Memory Footprint Calculation for Quantized LLMs
oneliner: Memory (bytes) = params × bits_per_param / 8, plus overhead for scales, KV-cache, and non-quantized components.
explanation: 70B at fp16 = 140 GB. At int4 = ~35 GB. Complications: embeddings + layer norms usually kept at fp16 (~0.5-1 GB). Scale factors add ~0.125 bpw overhead at g=128. KV-cache grows with sequence length and can exceed model size (70B Llama-2 at 4096 tokens = ~2.5 GB per sequence; at batch 32 = 80 GB). Token generation throughput ≈ memory_bandwidth / model_weight_bytes.
fundamentals: Weight memory = P × b / 8. Scale overhead = P × (s_bits / g) / 8. KV-cache = 2 × layers × kv_heads × head_dim × seq_len × batch × kv_bytes. Practical: RTX 4090 (24 GB) fits 7B at fp16, 13B at int8, ~33B at int4. A100-80GB fits 70B at int8. H100 at 3.35 TB/s: 70B int4 ≈ 55 tok/s, 70B fp16 ≈ 14 tok/s.
sources: Pope et al., 'Efficiently Scaling Transformer Inference,' MLSys 2023, arXiv:2211.05102

foundational_papers.title: Efficiently Scaling Transformer Inference

name: GPTQ
expansion: GPT-Quantization (named after target: Generative Pre-trained Transformers)
oneliner: Post-training weight quantization using second-order (Hessian) information to compress weights to 3-4 bits with minimal accuracy loss.
explanation: Works layer-by-layer: quantizing one weight badly can be corrected by adjusting remaining weights. The Hessian (H = 2XX^T from calibration data) tells exactly how. Descends from Optimal Brain Surgeon → Optimal Brain Quantization. GPTQ's insight: for large models, greedy ordering is unnecessary — process columns left-to-right in blocks of 128. Uses Cholesky decomposition of inverse Hessian. Quantizes 175B model in ~4 GPU-hours. 128 calibration samples.
fundamentals: Objective: min ||WX - Q(W)X||^2. OBS update: when w_q is quantized, distribute error across remaining weights via δw = -δ × (H^{-1}_{:,q} / H^{-1}_{q,q}). GPTQ processes columns left-to-right with lazy batch updates per 128-column block. Complexity: O(d_row × d_col^2) vs OBQ's O(d_row × d_col^3).

foundational_papers.title: GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-Trained Transformers

name: AWQ
oneliner: Identifies the most important ~1% of weight channels via activation magnitudes, then protects them with per-channel scaling.
explanation: Not all weights matter equally. AWQ measures importance by activation statistics: channels with consistently large activations amplify weight errors. Instead of mixed-precision (which kills hardware efficiency), AWQ multiplies important weight channels by scale s before quantization, divides corresponding activations by s. Mathematically equivalent but reduces relative quantization error for salient channels. Only needs forward-pass stats — fast (minutes for 70B), no gradients or Hessian.
fundamentals: 1) Calibrate: s_x(j) = mean(|X[:,j]|) per channel j. 2) Compute scaling: s(j) = s_x(j)^α, α found by grid search minimizing ||Q(W·diag(s))·(X/diag(s)) - WX||. 3) Store W' = W·diag(s), quantize W'. 4) At runtime: activations divided by s (fused into preceding LayerNorm).

foundational_papers.title: AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration

name: EXL2
oneliner: Variable-bitrate format mixing 2-8 bit precision across layers — target any average bpw (e.g., 3.5, 4.65). Best quality-per-bit on consumer GPUs.
explanation: Native to ExLlamaV2 inference library. Unlike uniform-bitwidth methods, EXL2 allocates more bits to sensitive layers and fewer to robust ones. Two-pass: 1) Measurement — quantize every layer at multiple bitwidths, record error. 2) Solve — constrained optimization to minimize max error subject to target size. Can also mix bitwidths within a single layer across column groups. Based on GPTQ-style Hessian-guided rounding.
fundamentals: Measurement: quantize each layer at 2,3,4,5,6,8 bits, record ||WX - Q_b(W)X||^2. Solving: min max_i error_i(b_i) s.t. Σ size_i(b_i) = target. Column-level mixing via Hessian diagonal (proxy for column importance). Validation: post-quant perplexity < 30 = success.
sources: github.com/turboderp-org/exllamav2 (no paper; documented in repo wiki)

name: BitsAndBytes (bnb)
oneliner: The library behind LLM.int8() and QLoRA. Offers 8-bit (absmax) and 4-bit (nf4/fp4) quantization with deep HuggingFace integration.
explanation: Two main modes. 8-bit (LLM.int8()): vector-wise absmax quantization with mixed-precision decomposition — outlier features (>~6σ) kept in fp16, rest in int8. Solved the activation outlier problem. 4-bit (QLoRA): loads base model in nf4/fp4, trains LoRA adapters in fp16. Double quantization compresses scale factors. Tightly integrated with HuggingFace Transformers (load_in_8bit/load_in_4bit). Primary use: memory-efficient fine-tuning, not production serving.
fundamentals: 8-bit: per-vector absmax scaling. Outlier decomposition: X = X_normal + X_outlier, compute separately in int8 and fp16, sum results. 4-bit: per-group (g=64 default) with nf4 or fp4 dtype. Double quant: group the fp32 absmax values into blocks of 256, quantize to fp8 with their own absmax.

foundational_papers.title: LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale
QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models

name: HQQ
oneliner: Zero-calibration quantization — no forward passes through the model needed. Uses optimization on weight statistics alone. Supports 1-8 bit.
explanation: Most quantization methods (GPTQ, AWQ) need calibration data run through the model. HQQ reformulates quantization as a half-quadratic optimization problem that operates directly on weight tensors without any data. This makes it extremely fast and eliminates calibration-related failure modes. Quality is competitive with GPTQ/AWQ at 4-bit and somewhat worse at lower bitwidths. Main appeal: speed and simplicity.
fundamentals: Treats quantization as proximal optimization: split the problem into a rounding step and a scale-fitting step, alternate. No activations or Hessian needed — operates purely on weight statistics. Supports arbitrary group sizes and asymmetric quantization.

foundational_papers.title: Half-Quadratic Quantization of Large Language Models

name: AQLM
oneliner: Extreme compression (2-bit) using learned additive codebooks — highest quality at very low bitwidths.
explanation: Represents each weight group as a sum of entries from multiple small codebooks (additive vector quantization). A group of weights is encoded as a sum of M codewords from M codebooks of size 2^B each. For M=2, B=8: 2 bytes per group encodes a lookup into 256-entry codebooks that are jointly learned to minimize reconstruction error. Dominates at 2-bit quantization where simpler methods fail. Slow to quantize (hours) but inference is efficient with custom CUDA kernels.
fundamentals: For a group of K weights: w ≈ c1[i1] + c2[i2] + ... + cM[iM] where c_m are learned codebooks and i_m are indices stored as compact integers. Training alternates: fix codebooks, find best indices; fix indices, optimize codebooks. Effective bpw depends on M, B, K parameters.

foundational_papers.title: AQLM: Extreme Compression of Language Models via Additive Quantization

name: QuIP#
oneliner: 2-bit quantization using random rotations to spread information evenly across weights, then vector quantization with E8 lattice codebooks.
explanation: Key insight: quantization works best when weight matrices are 'incoherent' — no single weight is disproportionately important. QuIP# applies random orthogonal rotations (Hadamard transforms) to make weights incoherent, then uses lattice-based vector quantization (E8 lattice, a mathematically optimal packing) for extreme compression. Achieves viable 2-bit quantization for 70B+ models. Research-oriented; less ecosystem support than GPTQ/AWQ.
fundamentals: 1) Rotate weights: W' = UWV^T (random orthogonal U, V from Hadamard matrices). 2) Vector quantize rotated weights using E8 lattice codebook. 3) At inference: decode codebook entries and apply inverse rotations. The rotation is the key — it makes all weights roughly equally important, so uniform quantization works better.

foundational_papers.title: QuIP#: Even Better LLM Quantization with Hadamard Incoherence and Lattice Codebooks

name: EETQ
expansion: Easy and Efficient Quantization for Transformers
oneliner: Simple 8-bit weight-only quantization — drop-in replacement with minimal setup. No calibration needed.
explanation: A straightforward per-channel int8 weight quantization library. No calibration data, no complex algorithms — just absmax scaling per output channel. Trades off accuracy optimization (no Hessian, no activation-awareness) for simplicity and speed. Works well for int8 where the quality gap between methods is small. Integrates with HuggingFace Transformers.
fundamentals: Per-channel symmetric quantization: scale_j = max(|W[j,:]|) / 127. W_int8[j,:] = round(W[j,:] / scale_j). At inference: dequantize to fp16 and compute. That's it.

name: Quanto (optimum-quanto)
oneliner: HuggingFace's own quantization library — supports int2/int4/int8/float8 with a clean Pythonic API. Device-agnostic.
explanation: Quanto is designed as a simple, composable quantization toolkit. Supports both weight-only and weight+activation quantization. Key feature: device-agnostic — can quantize on CPU and run on GPU, supports CUDA, MPS (Apple), and XPU (Intel). Integrates directly with HuggingFace Transformers and diffusers. Less optimized for raw throughput than GPTQ/AWQ but easier to use and more flexible.
fundamentals: Provides quantized tensor types that wrap standard PyTorch tensors with scale/zero-point metadata. Quantization is eager (happens at model load time). Supports symmetric and affine quantization, per-tensor and per-axis granularity.

name: Marlin
expansion: Mixed-Precision Auto-Regressive Parallel Inference on GPUs
oneliner: Not a quantization method — a GPU kernel that makes GPTQ/AWQ inference fast. Fuses dequantization with matrix multiply.
explanation: Marlin is a CUDA kernel designed specifically for W4A16 inference. It reads int4 weights from memory, dequantizes in shared memory, and performs the fp16 GEMM in a single fused operation. This avoids materializing the full fp16 weight matrix and maximizes memory bandwidth utilization. Used internally by vLLM for GPTQ and AWQ models. Achieves near-theoretical-maximum memory bandwidth utilization (>90% on A100/H100).
fundamentals: Key optimizations: 1) Async global→shared memory copy of packed int4 weights. 2) Dequantize in shared memory using scale factors. 3) Feed into Tensor Core WMMA operations. 4) Asynchronous pipelining of load and compute stages. Achieves 4× throughput of naive fp16 GEMM for batch size 1.

foundational_papers.title: Marlin: Mixed-Precision Auto-Regressive Parallel INference on GPUs

name: imatrix / Importance Matrix
oneliner: Per-weight importance scores computed from calibration data — used by llama.cpp to weight quantization decisions. Powers IQ-quant methods.
explanation: An importance matrix records how much each weight contributes to the model's output. Computed by running calibration data through the model and accumulating squared activation magnitudes per weight position. Weights with high importance scores get quantized more carefully (in IQ methods, they get mapped to closer codebook entries). Importance matrices are what distinguish IQ-quants from Q-quants in llama.cpp — same bitwidth, but IQ uses importance data to make smarter rounding decisions.
fundamentals: For each layer with input X: importance[i,j] ∝ Σ_samples (X[:,j])^2. This approximates the diagonal of the Hessian — weights multiplied by large activations matter more. The imatrix is stored as a file and loaded during quantization. IQ-quant codebook assignment is weighted by these scores.
sources: llama.cpp documentation and source code, github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp

name: Unsloth Dynamic (UD)
oneliner: Proprietary layer-selective quantization that varies precision per layer based on sensitivity analysis. 10-30% KL divergence reduction vs uniform.
explanation: Not all layers are equally sensitive to quantization. UD profiles each layer's sensitivity using 300K-1.5M calibration tokens (varies by model size), then assigns higher precision to sensitive layers (typically attention layers near start/end of network) and lower precision to robust ones. Available for GGUF (UD- prefix on quant files) and MLX (UD-MLX in repo name). Claims 10-30% KL divergence reduction across quantization types vs uniform approaches.
fundamentals: 1) Profile: quantize each layer at multiple precisions, measure KL divergence from full-precision output. 2) Allocate: assign precision per layer to minimize total KL divergence within a size budget. 3) Quantize: apply chosen precision per layer. Similar in spirit to EXL2's approach but with different optimization objective (KL divergence vs reconstruction error).

name: SpinQuant
oneliner: Learns optimal rotation matrices during training so that rotated weights are easier to quantize — a QAT approach using geometric transformations.
explanation: Similar insight to QuIP# (rotations make weights more quantization-friendly), but instead of random rotations, SpinQuant LEARNS the optimal rotations during a fine-tuning phase. This means the model actively optimizes its internal representations to be amenable to low-bit quantization. Combines the geometric insight of incoherence processing with the training-time adaptation of QAT. Published by Meta researchers.
fundamentals: During fine-tuning: insert learnable rotation matrices R before quantization in each layer. Forward pass: W' = R @ W, then quantize W'. Gradients flow through to both W and R via STE. The rotations R converge to transformations that spread weight information evenly, minimizing quantization error.

foundational_papers.title: SpinQuant: LLM Quantization with Learned Rotations

name: GGUF
oneliner: The standard single-file format for llama.cpp/Ollama/LM Studio — contains model weights, metadata, tokenizer, and quantization info in one file.
explanation: Replaced GGML format in August 2023. Self-contained: everything needed to load and run a model is in one file. Supports any quantization type (Q2_K through Q8_0, IQ variants, UD variants, BF16). Metadata is key-value pairs (architecture, context length, vocab size, etc.) stored in the header. Designed for CPU + GPU split inference — can mmap weights and offload specific layers to GPU. Each GGUF repo on HuggingFace typically contains multiple files at different quant levels.
fundamentals: File structure: 1) Magic number ('GGUF'). 2) Version. 3) Tensor count. 4) Metadata KV pairs (architecture, tokenizer, quant type, etc.). 5) Tensor descriptors (name, shape, type, offset). 6) Tensor data (aligned, mmap-friendly). Quantization type is per-tensor — different tensors can use different types. Supports memory mapping for efficient loading.

name: safetensors
oneliner: HuggingFace's secure, fast tensor serialization — replaces pickle-based .bin files. Immune to arbitrary code execution.
explanation: Created because PyTorch's default serialization uses Python pickle, which can execute arbitrary code when loading. A malicious .bin file could run any code on your machine. safetensors stores tensors as raw bytes with a JSON header — no code execution possible. Also faster: supports memory mapping and lazy loading (load only the tensors you need). Now the default format on HuggingFace Hub. Used by GPTQ, AWQ, EXL2, and full-precision model uploads.
fundamentals: File structure: 8-byte header length, JSON header (tensor names, shapes, dtypes, offsets), raw tensor data. No nested structures, no code, no pickle. Can be memory-mapped for zero-copy loading. Validation: can verify file integrity without loading all data.

name: GGML
expansion: Georgi Gerganov Machine Learning (library and legacy format)
oneliner: Legacy format — predecessor to GGUF. No longer used for new models but you may encounter old repos with .ggml files.
explanation: GGML was the original format used by llama.cpp before GGUF. It lacked proper metadata support (no tokenizer info, architecture details, or versioning), making it fragile and hard to extend. GGUF replaced it in August 2023, adding self-describing metadata. The GGML C library itself is still actively used (it's the tensor math backend for llama.cpp), but the file format is obsolete.
fundamentals: Simple binary layout: tensor data with minimal headers. No standardized metadata — required external configuration files. No versioning — breaking changes required new tooling. GGUF solved all of these by adding a proper header with KV metadata.

name: K-Quants (Q2_K through Q8_K)
oneliner: llama.cpp's standard quantization types — hierarchical super-blocks of 256 elements with mixed-precision sub-block scales.
explanation: Designed by Iwan Kawrakow (PR #1684). The 'K' does NOT stand for K-means — it's a custom method. Structure: super-blocks of 256 elements, divided into sub-blocks. Each sub-block has its own quantized scale/min. The S/M/L suffixes (e.g., Q4_K_S, Q4_K_M, Q4_K_L) control how many bits are spent on scales vs data. Q4_K_M is the most popular default: good balance of quality and size. Range: Q2_K (~2.6 bpw) through Q8_K (~8.5 bpw).
fundamentals: Q4_K_M example: super-block of 256 values, split into 8 sub-blocks of 32. Each sub-block: 32 × 4-bit values + 6-bit scale + 6-bit min. Super-block has fp16 master scale and fp16 master min. Effective bpw: 4 + (6+6)/32 + (16+16)/256 ≈ 4.5 bpw. S tier: fewer scale bits, smaller. M tier: balanced. L tier: more scale bits, larger/better.

name: IQ-Quants (IQ1 through IQ4)
oneliner: llama.cpp's codebook/lookup-table quantization — uses importance matrices to make smarter rounding decisions. Better than K-quants at same bitwidth.
explanation: IQ-quants use vector quantization with codebooks instead of simple scalar rounding. An importance matrix (imatrix) weights which values matter most. The encoder maps groups of weights to the nearest codebook entry, weighted by importance. Higher quality than K-quants at the same bitwidth but slower to generate (needs imatrix computation). Particularly valuable at low bitwidths (IQ2, IQ3) where the quality gap vs K-quants is largest.
fundamentals: Codebook approach: a fixed set of representative vectors (codebook entries). Each group of weights is mapped to the nearest codebook entry. With importance weighting: minimize Σ importance[i] × (w[i] - codebook[nearest][i])^2 instead of unweighted MSE. IQ4_XS at ~4.25 bpw outperforms Q4_K_S at ~4.5 bpw.

name: MXFP4_MOE
expansion: Microscaling FP4 optimized for Mixture-of-Experts layers
oneliner: E2M1 micro-float with block scaling, specifically targeting MoE expert weights. Only appears in 26B-A4B GGUF repos.
explanation: Applies the OCP Microscaling FP4 format (E2M1 elements with shared E8M0 block scales) specifically to the expert weight matrices in Mixture-of-Experts models. MoE expert weights are a good target because they are numerous (128 experts) but individually small, and the block-scaling approach handles their weight distributions well. Relatively new format in llama.cpp.
fundamentals: Same as E2M1/MXFP4: 32 elements share one 8-bit scale. Per-element: 1 sign, 2 exp, 1 mantissa = 15 distinct values. Effective 4.25 bpw. The _MOE suffix is an architecture annotation, not a different quantization algorithm.
